source_paper_title: Generalization Bounds in the Predict-Then-Optimize Framework
source_paper_bibtex: @article{El_Balghiti_2023, title={Generalization Bounds in the Predict-Then-Optimize Framework}, volume={48}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.1330}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.1330}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={El Balghiti, Othman and Elmachtoub, Adam N. and Grigas, Paul and Tewari, Ambuj}, year={2023}, month=nov, pages={2043–2065} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (discussion following margin-based bounds and contraction lemmas); reiterated on page 8, start of Section 4.2 (margin-based generalization bounds).

Even though we construct a Lipschitz upper bound on SPO loss in a general norm setting (Theorem 3), our margin bounds (Theorem 4) are stated in the \(\ell_2\) norm setting. This is because the most general contraction type lemma for vector valued Lipschitz functions we know of only works for the \(\ell_2\)-norm [17]. Similar results are available in the infinity norm setting [3] but our understanding of general norms appears limited at present.

To the best of our knowledge, extending the vector-contraction inequality of [17] to an arbitrary norm setting (or even the case of general \(\ell_q\)-norms) remains an open question that would have interesting applications to our framework.
title: Extend vector-contraction inequalities for Rademacher complexity to general norms
background_latex:
Let \(\mathcal X\) be an input (feature) space and \(\mathcal H\) be a class of vector-valued hypotheses \(f:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R^d\). Fix an i.i.d. sample \(x_1,\dots,x_n\in\mathcal X\). The (multivariate) empirical Rademacher complexity of \(\mathcal H\) is
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H):=\mathbb E_{\sigma}\Big[\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma_i^\top f(x_i)\Big],
\]
where \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n\in\{\pm1\}^d\) have i.i.d. Rademacher coordinates and \(\mathbb E_\sigma\) denotes expectation over these signs.

Let \(\|\cdot\|\) be a norm on \(\mathbb R^d\). Consider a real-valued function \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) that is \(L\)-Lipschitz with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\), i.e.,
\[
|\phi(u)-\phi(v)|\le L\,\|u-v\|\quad\text{for all }u,v\in\mathbb R^d.
\]
Define the composed scalar class \(\phi\circ\mathcal H:=\{x\mapsto \phi(f(x)):\ f\in\mathcal H\}\). Its empirical Rademacher complexity is
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H):=\mathbb E_{\epsilon}\Big[\sup_{f\in\mathcal H}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \epsilon_i\,\phi(f(x_i))\Big],
\]
where \(\epsilon_1,\dots,\epsilon_n\in\{\pm1\}\) are i.i.d. Rademacher signs.

A vector-contraction inequality is a bound that controls \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\) in terms of \(L\) and \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)\) (or a closely related complexity), possibly with a constant depending on the choice of norm \(\|\cdot\|\). Known general results (e.g., Maurer 2016) provide such inequalities in the case \(\|\cdot\|=\|\cdot\|_2\), and some related results exist for \(\|\cdot\|_\infty\), but general norms (including general \(\ell_q\) norms) are not covered by the currently available contraction tools referenced by the paper.
problem_definition_latex:
Develop a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity for Lipschitz maps under a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) on \(\mathbb R^d\): find conditions and an explicit bound of the form
\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)\,L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)
\]
(or an analogous bound in terms of a suitable multivariate Rademacher complexity), where \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) is \(L\)-Lipschitz with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\), \(\mathcal H\subseteq\{f:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R^d\}\), and \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) is an explicit constant or function of the norm, extending the known \(\ell_2\)-based vector-contraction inequality to general norms (including general \(\ell_q\) norms).
latex:

\[
\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)\,L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)

location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (discussion following margin-based bounds and contraction lemmas); reiterated on page 8, start of Section 4.2 (margin-based generalization bounds).
open_problem_quote:
Even though we construct a Lipschitz upper bound on SPO loss in a general norm setting (Theorem 3), our margin bounds (Theorem 4) are stated in the \(\ell_2\) norm setting. This is because the most general contraction type lemma for vector valued Lipschitz functions we know of only works for the \(\ell_2\)-norm [17]. Similar results are available in the infinity norm setting [3] but our understanding of general norms appears limited at present.

To the best of our knowledge, extending the vector-contraction inequality of [17] to an arbitrary norm setting (or even the case of general \(\ell_q\)-norms) remains an open question that would have interesting applications to our framework.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for a general inequality holding uniformly over function classes and Lipschitz maps, rather than for existence/nonexistence on a finite instance family. Automated search might find toy counterexamples for candidate bounds, but the relevant quantifiers and norm-dependent constants make systematic finite search unlikely to be decisive.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could explore candidate inequalities by proposing norm-dependent constants and testing them empirically on randomized finite-dimensional hypothesis classes and Lipschitz functions to search for violations or plausible sharp constants. However, the ultimate goal is a universal proof (or a rigorous counterexample construction), so computational feedback would be only a heuristic guide.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the NeurIPS 2019 SPO generalization paper, the margin-based bounds for strongly convex feasible regions hinge on showing that the \(\gamma\)-margin SPO loss is Lipschitz in the dual norm \(\|\cdot\|_*\) (Theorem 3), and then converting this Lipschitz property into a Rademacher complexity bound via Maurer’s vector-contraction inequality. The authors explicitly restrict the final contraction step to the \(\ell_2\) setting (Theorem 4) because the most general available vector contraction tool they cite (Maurer 2016) is Euclidean; they note only partial analogues for \(\ell_\infty\) and leave general norms (including \(\ell_q\)) open.

The forward-citing literature provided here does not resolve this contraction question. Instead, multiple subsequent predict-then-optimize and learning-to-optimize works (e.g., Integrated conditional estimation-optimization; Decision-focused learning with directional gradients; End-to-end learning to warm-start for real-time quadratic optimization; Risk bounds and calibration for a smart predict-then-optimize method) continue to formulate Lipschitzness and stability in \(\ell_2\) precisely so that Maurer’s \(\ell_2\) vector contraction can be applied. This pattern reinforces that a norm-dependent contraction inequality \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)L\,\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)\) would have immediate impact by allowing analyses to align with the natural geometry of the decision/parameter spaces.

On the tools side, work on uniform convexity/smoothness of norm balls (Local and global uniform convexity conditions) suggests a plausible route: in Banach-space language, contraction constants typically depend on type/cotype or uniform smoothness parameters of \((\mathbb R^d,\|\cdot\|)\) and its dual. However, none of the cited papers actually prove a Maurer-style vector contraction inequality for general norms or provide explicit constants for \(\ell_q\) beyond the known special cases. Thus, based on the provided forward citations, the problem remains open; promising directions include leveraging Banach-space type/smoothness (especially for \(\ell_q\), Schatten-\(q\), and group norms) to obtain explicit \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) and identifying the right multivariate complexity notion that matches \(\|\cdot\|\) and \(\|\cdot\|_*\).

sources.title: Integrated conditional estimation-optimization
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a finite-sample generalization bound for its integrated conditional estimation-optimization (ICEO) method by combining (a) Lipschitzness of a regularized optimization oracle and (b) Maurer’s (2016) 
vector contraction inequality
 for 
\(\ell_2\)-Lipschitz maps. Concretely, under Assumption 3 (\(c(\cdot)\) is \(L_c\)-Lipschitz in \(w\) and \(\phi\) is 1-strongly convex), Proposition 2 shows \(w_\rho\) is \((L_c/\rho)\)-Lipschitz in \(p\) (in \(\ell_2\)), implying \(c\circ w_\rho\) is \((L_c^2/\rho)\)-Lipschitz. Then Theorem 3 states that for any \(\delta\in(0,1]\) and \(\rho_n>0\), with prob. \(\ge 1-\delta\), for all \(f\in\mathcal H\):
\[
R(w_{\rho_n}\circ f)\le \widehat R_n(w_{\rho_n}\circ f)+\sqrt{2}\,\frac{L_c^2}{\rho_n}\,R_n(\mathcal H)+\bar c\sqrt{\frac{\log(1/\delta)}{2n}}.
\]
The step bounding \(R_n(c\circ w_{\rho_n}\circ\mathcal H)\) by \(\sqrt{2}(L_c^2/\rho_n)R_n(\mathcal H)\) explicitly invokes Maurer (2016), which is an \(\ell_2\)-based vector contraction result.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity when \(\phi\) is Lipschitz w.r.t. a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) on \(\mathbb R^d\), extending Maurer (2016) beyond \(\ell_2\) (and some \(\ell_\infty\) cases). This paper does not develop such a general-norm contraction inequality; instead it stays within the \(\ell_2\) framework precisely to apply Maurer (2016). It even notes (Assumption 3.A) that \(c(\cdot)\) is Lipschitz in output measured by \(\|\cdot\|_2\), and then applies Maurer’s \(\ell_2\)-based contraction to relate the scalar Rademacher complexity of the composed loss class \(c\circ w_\rho\circ\mathcal H\) to the multivariate Rademacher complexity of \(\mathcal H\). Thus the paper provides additional motivation and an application context where a general-norm contraction would be useful (since decisions are measured in a generic norm \(\|\cdot\|\) but outputs are forced into \(\ell_2\) to use existing tools), but it does not resolve the open problem nor extend the inequality to general norms \(\ell_q\), etc.

sources.title: Decision-focused learning with directional gradients
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves uniform approximation/generalization bounds for its perturbation-gradient surrogate losses in predict-then-optimize. In particular (Theorem 3.4), under boundedness and a Lipschitz log-conditional density assumption, for any step size h with 0<h<1/L, with probability at least 1-\delta\),
\[
\sup_{f\in\mathcal F}\Big|\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \hat\ell_h^b(f(X_i),Y_i)-\mathbb E[\ell(f(X),Y)]\Big| \lesssim BLh + \frac{B^2}{h}\,\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal F) + B\sqrt{\tfrac{\log(1/\delta)}{n}},
\]
where \(\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal F)=\mathbb E[\sup_{f\in\mathcal F}\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma_i^\top f(X_i)]\) is the multivariate Rademacher complexity. They obtain excess-regret bounds (Theorem 3.8) such as \(\mathrm{ERegret}(\hat f_h)\lesssim \sqrt{B^3L\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal F)}+\sqrt{B/n}\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity under general norms \(\|\cdot\|\) (beyond \(\ell_2\), \(\ell_\infty\)). This paper explicitly uses Maurer (2016) vector contraction to control the Rademacher complexity of a composed class arising from a Lipschitz surrogate loss (see proof of Theorem 3.4), but it does so with the Euclidean norm \(\|\cdot\|_2\): Assumption 1.1 bounds \(\|z\|_2\), Lemma 2.1 gives Lipschitzness in \(\|\cdot\|_2\), and the contraction step yields a factor \(\propto (B/h)\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal F)\). The paper does not develop a contraction inequality for a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) nor identify an explicit constant \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) for \(\ell_q\) norms. Still, it provides a concrete application context where such a general-norm contraction would immediately extend their bounds (e.g., if decisions are bounded in a non-Euclidean norm or if one wants Lipschitzness w.r.t. \(\|\cdot\|_q\)).

sources.title: Local and global uniform convexity conditions
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops quantitative equivalences between uniform convexity/smoothness of norm balls (and their gauges/support functions) and scaling inequalities. In particular, for a centrally symmetric convex body (norm ball) \(C\subset\mathbb R^m\) and \(p\ge2\), \(q\in(1,2]\) with \(1/p+1/q=1\), Theorem 4.1 shows equivalence between: (i) \((\alpha,p)\)-uniform convexity of \(C\) (modulus \(\delta_{\|\cdot\|_C}(\varepsilon)\ge \alpha\varepsilon^p\)); (ii) a global scaling inequality \(\langle d, y-x\rangle\ge \alpha\|d\|_{C^\circ}\,\|y-x\|_C^p\) for \(y\in\partial C\), \(d\in N_C(y)\); and (iii) uniform smoothness of the power of the support function \(\sigma_C^q\): there exists \(c\) such that \(\sigma_C^q\) is \((c,q)\)-uniformly smooth w.r.t. \(\|\cdot\|_{C^\circ}\). Theorem 5.1 gives local analogues at a boundary point/direction. Section 6.2 uses these to bound Rademacher complexity of low-norm linear predictors when the regularizer ball is uniformly convex.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity under a general norm \(\|\cdot\|\) for \(L\)-Lipschitz \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\). This paper does not prove such a contraction inequality. Instead it studies geometric/functional properties of general norm balls (uniform convexity/smoothness, duality, scaling inequalities) and derives a (scalar) Rademacher complexity bound for low-norm linear classes (Theorem 6.5) via Banach-space type. These results are potentially relevant tools because vector-contraction constants for general norms often depend on type/cotype or smoothness of the underlying Banach space; the paper supplies quantitative links between uniform convexity/smoothness and Rademacher type, but it does not address contraction for composing \(\phi\) with vector-valued classes \(\mathcal H\) nor provide a bound of the form \(\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\phi\circ\mathcal H)\le C(\|\cdot\|)L\widehat{\mathfrak R}_n(\mathcal H)\).

sources.title: Risk bounds and calibration for a smart predict-then-optimize method
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper’s main theoretical results are calibration/risk bounds for the SPO+ surrogate loss in predict-then-optimize. For a bounded polyhedral feasible set \(S\), it proves a uniform lower bound on the SPO+ calibration function of the form \(\hat\delta_{\ell_{\mathrm{SPO}+}}(\epsilon) \ge c\,\min\{\epsilon^2,\epsilon\}\) over a restricted distribution class (Theorem 3.1), yielding an excess SPO risk rate \(O(n^{-1/4})\) when combined with a generalization bound (Corollary 3.1). For \(S\) a level set of a \(\mu\)-strongly convex and \(L\)-smooth function with respect to a norm \(\|\cdot\|_A\), it proves linear calibration \(\hat\delta_{\ell_{\mathrm{SPO}+}}(\epsilon)\ge c'\,\epsilon\) (Theorem 4.1), yielding an excess SPO risk rate \(O(n^{-1/2})\) (Corollary 4.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity under general norms \(\|\cdot\|\), extending Maurer (2016) beyond \(\ell_2\). This paper does not develop new vector-contraction inequalities. It explicitly relies on Maurer’s \(\ell_2\)-based vector contraction to derive generalization/sample-complexity bounds for SPO+ (see discussion before Corollary 3.1 and Appendix A). While the paper uses generic norms and dual norms in its optimization/calibration analysis (e.g., Assumption 4.1 with a norm \(\|\cdot\|_A\)), it does not translate Lipschitzness under a general norm into Rademacher complexity bounds via a new contraction tool. Thus it provides motivation and an application context where such a general-norm contraction inequality would be useful, but it does not resolve the open problem.

sources.title: End-to-end learning to warm-start for real-time quadratic optimization
sources.theorem_or_result:
Assuming the Douglas–Rachford (DR) operator family \(T_\theta\) is contractive in \(\ell_2\), i.e. \(\|T_\theta(z)-T_\theta(w)\|_2\le \beta_\theta\|z-w\|_2\) with \(\beta:=\max_{\theta\in\Theta}\beta_\theta\in(0,1)\), they prove a generalization bound for end-to-end learned warm-starts: with probability \(\ge 1-\delta\), for all networks \(h_W\in\mathcal H\) satisfying \(\mathrm{dist}_{\mathrm{fix}\,T_\theta}(h_W(\theta))\le B\),
\[
R^k(h_W)\le \widehat R^k(h_W)+2\beta^k\Big(2\sqrt2\,\mathrm{erad}(\mathcal H)+\frac{3B\log(2/\delta)}{2N}\Big),
\]
where \(R^k\) is expected fixed-point residual after \(k\) DR iterations and \(\mathrm{erad}(\mathcal H)\) is multivariate empirical Rademacher complexity of the warm-start network class. The proof uses Maurer (2016, Cor. 4) vector-contraction in \(\ell_2\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for vector-contraction inequalities for empirical Rademacher complexity under general norms \(\|\cdot\|\) (e.g. \(\ell_q\)), extending Maurer’s \(\ell_2\) result. This paper does not extend contraction to other norms; instead it applies Maurer (2016) in \(\ell_2\) to bound a Rademacher term arising in a learning-to-warm-start analysis. The only Lipschitz/contraction property used is \(2\beta^k\)-Lipschitzness of \(\ell_\theta\circ T_\theta^k\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\), followed by Maurer’s \(\ell_2\) vector contraction. Thus it provides motivation and an application context where a general-norm contraction inequality would be useful, but it does not develop such an inequality.

sources.title: On learning latent models with multi-instance weak supervision
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves Rademacher-style generalization/error bounds for multi-instance partial-label learning under semantic loss and its top-k approximation. In particular, for a single classifier and known transition \(\sigma\) that is 1- and \(M\)-unambiguous, with probability \(\ge 1-\delta\):
\[
R_{01}(f) \le \Phi\Big((k+1)\big(\widehat R^k_P(f;\sigma;T_P) + 2\sqrt{k}M^{3/2} R_{Mm_P}(F) + \sqrt{\tfrac{\log(1/\delta)}{2m_P}}\big)\Big),
\]
where \(R_{Mm_P}(F)\) is a (vector-valued) Rademacher complexity of the scoring class \(F\) (Def. 8) and \(\Phi\) is increasing with \(\lim_{t\to 0}\Phi(t)/t=1\) (Theorem 2). Analogous bounds are given for multiple classifiers (Theorem 4) and for unknown transitions under an unambiguous transition space (Theorem 5).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a vector-contraction inequality for empirical Rademacher complexity for Lipschitz maps \(\phi:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) under general norms \(\|\cdot\|\), extending known \(\ell_2\) vector contraction results. This paper does use a contraction lemma (citing Cortes et al. 2016) to bound the Rademacher complexity of a composed loss class arising from semantic loss/top-\(k\) WMC, but:
- the contraction lemma employed is explicitly \(\ell_2\)-based (Lipschitz w.r.t. \(\|\cdot\|_2\)) and yields bounds in terms of an \(\ell_2\)-type vector Rademacher complexity;
- there is no development of contraction inequalities for general norms (e.g., \(\ell_q\) with \(q\ne 2,\infty\)), nor an explicit constant \(C(\|\cdot\|)\) depending on an arbitrary norm.
Thus it does not solve the stated open problem. At best, it is tangentially relevant as an application context where such generalized vector-contraction tools could tighten or generalize their Rademacher bounds for semantic loss under alternative norm/Lipschitz structures.
